title: Validation in Multi-Tier Applications
text: Validation in Multi-Tier Applications In multi-tier applications, validation is typically centralized in a business logic layer. Multi-tier applications separate interactive presentation of an application from business logic. The user interacts with forms on a presentation layer that have no access to database sources, and these forms interact with services that conduct application processing. These services in turn interact with a data layer through services that handle data integrity and validation. The number of layers and their functions varies between applications, but it is usual to have a presentation layer, application layer, and persistent data layer. The key features of validation in a multi-tier application are: Validation processes are centralized, ideally being placed in a business rules layer, for maintenance as well as performance reasons. In this type of architecture, it may not be desirable to make extensive use of declarative validation, because this: Shifts validation into the presentation layer Decentralizes validation logic, distributing it to components and making maintenance more complex Declarative validation is static and can generally only be changed by reprogramming Validation can be made dynamic because it is centralized. When a business rule changes, the associated validation condition can be changed in one place to reflect the new situation. The presentation layer software does not need to be changed or reinstalled.
